sn.s: S'Schwerschte a nere Sproch isch de Humor.
sn.e: The hardest thing in a language is the humour.

sn.s: Mer mues d'Wörter verstah und no öppis meh.
sn.e: You have to understand the words and something more.

sn.s: De Schwiizer Humor isch oft troche und liis.
sn.e: Swiss humour is often dry and quiet.

sn.s: Mer seit öppis ärnschthaft und meint's umkehrt.
sn.e: You say something seriously and mean the opposite.

sn.s: "Schöns Wätter" bi strömendem Räge isch en Witz.
sn.e: "Nice weather" in pouring rain is a joke.

sn.s: Mer mues zwüsche de Zile lose chönne.
sn.e: You have to be able to listen between the lines.

sn.s: Am Aafang han ich de Witz nöd immer gmerkt.
sn.e: At the start I didn't always catch the joke.

sn.s: Ich han ärnschthaft gantwortet, und alli händ glächlet.
sn.e: I answered seriously, and everyone smiled.

sn.s: Jetz ghör ich de fiin Underton.
sn.e: Now I hear the fine undertone.

sn.s: Und ich cha sälber so es Spässli mache.
sn.e: And I can make such a little joke myself.

sn.s: Wenn d'Lüt lached, denn ghör ich würkli derzue.
sn.e: When people laugh, then I really belong.

sn.s: Humor verbindet meh als alli korrekte Sätz.
sn.e: Humour connects more than all the correct sentences.

sn.s: Mer lacht nöd übereinand, sondern mitenand.
sn.e: You don't laugh at each other, but with each other.

sn.s: Und das isch s'schönschte Zeiche vo Zämeghörigkeit.
sn.e: And that's the loveliest sign of belonging.
